md: **Volume** is the amount of 3D space inside a closed shape, measured in *cubic units*. The recurring pattern: for prisms and cylinders, volume is **base area × height**. For pyramids and cones, volume is one-third of that. Spheres get their own special formula.
A **prism** has two parallel congruent bases (like the top and bottom of a box). For any prism:
where $B$ is the *area* of the base and $h$ is the *height* (perpendicular distance between the two bases).
**Cube** (side $s$): $V = s^3$. (Special case: square prism with all sides equal.)
**Rectangular prism** ($l \times w \times h$): $V = lwh$. (Base is a rectangle of area $lw$.)
A cylinder is essentially a 'circular prism'. Same formula:
where $r$ is the radius of the circular base and $h$ is the height. The base area $\pi r^2$ replaces the polygon-base area.

blocks.title: Pyramids and cones — divide by 3
blocks.body: Pyramids and cones are 'pointy' versions of prisms and cylinders — same base, but tapering to a point. Their volume is exactly **one-third** of the corresponding prism or cylinder. Why one-third? It can be proven with calculus, or by carefully fitting three pyramids together to fill a prism. Either way: $\boxed{V = \frac{1}{3}Bh}$.

blocks.title: How volume scales
blocks.body: Volume scales as the **cube** of the linear scale factor. Doubling all the dimensions of any 3D shape multiplies its volume by $2^3 = 8$. (This is why a small ant can fall safely from a great height, while a much heavier mouse cannot — surface area scales as $k^2$ but mass/volume as $k^3$.)

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Prism / cylinder**: $V = Bh$ (base area times height). **Pyramid / cone**: $V = \frac{1}{3}Bh$ (one-third of the prism). **Sphere**: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$. Volume scales as the cube of linear dimensions.
